body_html:
Page 1

Bearing 315
the newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club
NOV-DEC 1986

Trollhaugen

by Harvey Friedman

Everything went as expected for the Class "A" meet except the attendance. We had perfect weather (sunny, but not too hot to run), quite varied terrain, fine courses set by Sture Carlsson to challenge us (considering my slow times, 'twas quite a challenge), and even one third of last year's U.S. cross-country ski orienteering team (Larry & Sara Mae Berman and Ron Pontius).

Mike Schuh and Dave Enger spent nearly every weekend in May and June field-checking the map. They also worked with course-setter Sture Carlsson setting out markers and vetting the courses on the weekend of the meet. Mike was responsible for obtaining the $700 contribution from the Sons of Norway for production of the map, and still had time to help print courses, and enter results in the computer at the meet.

Gary McCausland built the start area and made all the signs, and helped with course-printing, and setting out markers; Arnold Kay spent many hours programming the computer and making awards before the meet, and operating the computer the weekend of the meet; Don Denbo (despite a badly swollen ankle that kept him out of the competition the second day) organized and trained a finely tuned finish crew.

Sture Carlsson spent many days designing and checking twelve courses, which required several trips to Snoqualmie Pass. Since the final map didn't get back from the printer until about 10 days before the meet, he had to do all of this with a bad color xerox of the field map.

Doug and Katie Sprugel handled pre-registration, and had to put up with a mailbox full of entries for the meet; Joe Abad and his family put together an excellent barbecue on Saturday evening.

Special thanks go to Larry and Sara Mae Berman from Cambridge, MA and Bob Anglin from L.A. who pitched in and worked as hard at taking up the dance stage as any of the COC members who worked on those 50 sheets of plywood.

Last, but certainly not least, thanks to everyone else who helped out: Eileen Williams, who coordinated the t-shirt printing; Robert Miller, who negotiated the permit fee with Burlington Northern, and who, with Sandy Miller, helped assemble the meet program. Roger Sharp, who did publicity for the meet; and Harvey Friedman, who got ads for the program. And everyone who helped on the days of the meet: Rick Breseman, Scott Coble, Ardis Dull, Bob Forbes, Mary Forbes, Harvey Friedman, Robert Miller, Sandy Kish Miller, Sal Munoz, Bob Rein, Erin Renshaw, Mike Tharp, Peggy Tharp, Rachel Tharp, and Anne York.

Page 2

B.C. Championships

Several members of Cascade and Nisqually Orienteering Clubs attended the British Columbia Orienteering Championships held on Vancouver Island on August 30 and 31. The first day was an individual event, and the second day was a relay. The weather was perfect for orienteering, not too hot and not too cold. Thetis Lake, the site of the individual event, is typical Pacific Northwest vegetation, in other words, salal, salal, and more salal.

Saturday's courses were challenging, especially control 3 on course 7, where several people had to dodge a nest of angry bees to get to the marker. Sunday's relay was held on the grounds of Royal Roads Military Academy, located on the shores of Esquimalt Lagoon about five miles from Victoria. The weather was again perfect for orienteering, with a cool breeze coming in off the lagoon. The forest was a bit more open than at Thetis, although there were some thick spots. Fortunately, no one encountered any bees.

The American contingent did pretty well in the standings, and it was an enjoyable weekend in Canada.

Page 4

Annual Meeting

This year's annual meeting and election of officers will be held on November 13th from 6:45-8:45 p.m. at the University Branch Library on the corner of N.E. 50th St. and Roosevelt. Mike Schuh will present a slide show about the Western U.S. Orienteering Championships (Nederland, Colorado), the B.C. Championships (Vancouver Island, B.C.) and the Washington State Championships (Trollhaugen).

Bus-O

In November, the Cascade Orienteering Club will hold a novel type of orienteering event: bus orienteering. Organized as a large scale score event, it will test the ability of the participants to use city street maps and the Metro bus system to navigate around the city.

Registration Information
Start and Finish will be at Westlake Mall (4th & Pine in downtown Seattle).

Schedule
8:30 - 9:30 a.m. Registration 9:45 a.m. Last minute instructions and announcements
10:00 a.m. Mass start 1:30 p.m. Return by this time to avoid penalty points
2:00 p.m. All entrants must be back at Westlake Mall 2:15 p.m. Awards Ceremony

Entry Fees
$2.00 Individual $2.00 Wheelchair (wheelchair entrants should have an assistant)
* $3.00 Team (2 or 3 people per team; only 1 wheelchair per team)

Bus Fare
In addition to the registration fee, participants must supply their own bus fare. You may purchase an all-day pass for $1.00 from any bus driver. If you have a regular Metro pass, you may use it.

Instructions
Your objective is to accumulate as many points as possible within the time limit. Each control is assigned a point value based on its distance and/or difficulty. It is usually not possible to visit every control within the time limit, so you must choose which points to visit. You may visit the controls in any order. Penalty points will be assessed if you return past the time limit.

Page 5

  1. You will be given a list of control descriptions. Each control description will consist of the name and address of a retail business in Seattle. You will also be asked to answer some question about the business in addition to punching in.
  2. You must either walk/run or use a bus to get from one control point to the next.
  3. You must return to the Finish area by 2:00 p.m. Penalty points will be assessed for anyone returning after 1:30 p.m.
  4. Your score will be figured by adding the points from each control you visited. The person or team with the highest total will be declared the winner in each division.
  5. This event is being run to gain publicity for orienteering and for the Cascade Orienteering Club. Therefore, one extra rule: no team may have more than one orienteering club member on it.

For additional information, call Mike Schuh at 783-3960.

Expiration

Please check the date on the mailing label to see when your membership expires. Renew promptly if you want to continue receiving Bearing 315. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Pat Albright, Kurt Blackstad, Jo Brown, Georgie Friedenberg, Judy Hill family, Jack Raidy, Melanie Wheeler, and P.M. Greenspan.

Some members of Ellensburg and Sammamish Orienteering Clubs purchase "newsletter only" memberships from COC. These people should renew their memberships through their own clubs rather than through COC.

Information

For those of you who want to find out about orienteering meets in other parts of the country, Cascade exchanges newsletters with clubs in New England, New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Delaware, Alabama, Kansas, Missouri, Texas, Colorado, Oregon, California, and British Columbia, Canada. Call Harvey Friedman (784-2774) if you want to borrow a newsletter to read about other clubs, or if you want current information from them.

Page 6

Trollhaugen - Another View

by Roger Sharp

Trollhaugen: Norwegian for "troll hill." I didn't see the trolls. I saw the hills. At the awards ceremony on Sunday afternoon, Liv Halvorsen was reported as saying "this isn't orienteering" when she had finished her course. Maybe she saw the trolls.

There was some deadfall, grassy fields, underbrush and dry river beds. Dry river beds in the Cascades! Near the wettest city in the country! This is a part of the Cascades new to me. It is, however, just as scenic and wild as the rest. Several people saw deer (probably SOC's Bob Rein bounding down a hillside). At one control was a beautiful waterfall. I saw an abundance of elk sign. You see a lot when you're on the course for three hours. Can you imagine people rushing through all this beauty in less than an hour? Crazy.

Even crazier was doing it again the next day. I didn't talk to one person on Day 2 who wasn't sore. Oh, yes I did. Alexander Rybak. Five months old, hanging out in Mom's front snuggle pack. Actually, he might have been sore. He didn't say.

About 110 people pre-registered for Trollhaugen. A lot of people registered on the day of the race. I saw many people milling about the popular spots: the free refreshments, the results board, the barbecue, the portable toilets. For people who like statistics:

__PH_0__ 35% were COC members
24% were Canadians 19% were Washingtonians from outside the Puget Sound area
8% were from the U.S. outside of Washington Participants represented 17 orienteering clubs
* Two people were injured on the course (sprained ankles)

Sara Mae and Larry Berman, publishers of Orienteering North America magazine, each took home a trophy. I heard they were interested by the news of snow at the Nisqually O' Club's Tenalquot meet in February of this year. Though the weather at Trollhaugen was warm and sunny, if they had stayed two more days they may have seen snow in Snoqualmie Pass.

Colorful club president Robert Miller presented the awards on Sunday, his mind tripping over Sweden and Norway each time he informed the audience where certain winners were from. Quick as he is, he caught on to his little glitch and introduced Sara Mae Berman as "from wherever it is back east." This is not a direct quote, but it may as well be.

All in all, the meet went as smoothly as contour lines printed on a map, and I'm sure meet director Debbie Newell is happy.

Page 7

LETTERS TO THE EDITOR

Dear Editor,

I'd like everyone to thank Debbie Newell for the job she did putting out the superb last issue of the COC newsletter and hope that all the others she does next year as regular newsletter editor turn out as well. We need more people to step forward and offer to accomplish things for the club.

Most of the membership of COC enjoys participating in o' meets; few members enjoy the administrative aspects of the sport. However, orienteering in this country is a sport run by volunteers, which means people are needed to design courses, arrange for maps to be made, schedule events, register, time, and teach others how to do the above. Volunteers can learn new skills, make new friends, and share the camaraderie of putting on a well-attended, successful meet. You don't have to be a "good" or proficient orienteer, just enthusiastic. Several returned surveys from last spring had comments about the officers being "elitist" or cliquish; those comments hurt those who worked at meets or who served on the board. If you want proof that they are not cliquish, just volunteer to help out and see how eagerly you will be welcomed. Newer members can learn from more experienced ones how to do most of the above-mentioned jobs, but we dearly need someone (anyone competent) to help the current publicity coordinator get our schedule published REGULARLY in the local media (particularly the Times and the P.I.). We need this ongoing name familiarity so that the club can grow. Help the soon-to-be-elected board members. Better yet, be one. If you enjoy the sport, please volunteer to help out.

I would rather see 100 or more people come to each meet and have 75 treat it as a 10km run and just leave afterward than have only 25 friendly people show up. I feel that if we can introduce enough people to the sport, some will stay and become involved. A big problem is keeping the interest of those who do come fairly often. To this end, I think that the four clubs in the state of Washington should coordinate their schedules so that in each and every month of the year there will be at least one meet on a 1:2500 to 1:5000 scale map, primarily to introduce beginners to the sport in a non-threatening locale and also at least one meet on a 1:10,000 to 1:24,000 scale map, so that the non-competitive but no-longer beginners won't become bored and drop out. If anyone agrees with anything I've advocated here, come to the annual meeting in November and make your voice heard.

Harvey Friedman
Membership Coordinator
Former President &
Former Newsletter Editor

THERE IS A HEAVEN FOR VOLUNTEERS

Many will be shocked to find
When the day of judgement nears
That there's a special place in Heaven
Set aside for volunteers

Furnished with big recliners
Satin couches and footstools
Where there's no Committee Chairman
No group leaders, no car pools

No eager team that needs a coach
No bazaar and no bake sale
There will be nothing to staple
Not one thing to fold or mail

Telephone lists will be outlawed
But a finger snap will bring
Cool drinks and gourmet dinners
And rare treats fit for a king

You ask "Who'll serve those privileged few
And work for all they're worth?"
Why, all those who reaped the benefits
And not once volunteered on Earth

-author unknown

TROLLHAUGEN T-SHIRTS
Several Trollhaugen t-shirts remain for those of you who didn't get one. We are offering a special post-meet rate of $6.00 apiece for these lovely aqua shirts emblazoned with an orienteering troll. To purchase a shirt, please call Debbie Newell at (206) 488-7471.

block_index.text: Bearing 315 the newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club NOV-DEC 1986

Everything went as expected for the Class "A" meet except the attendance. We had perfect weather (sunny, but not too hot to run), quite varied terrain, fine courses set by Sture Carlsson to challenge us (considering my slow times, 'twas quite a challenge), and even one third of last year's U.S. cross-country ski orienteering team (Larry & Sara Mae Berman and Ron Pontius).

Mike Schuh and Dave Enger spent nearly every weekend in May and June field-checking the map. They also worked with course-setter Sture Carlsson setting out markers and vetting the courses on the weekend of the meet. Mike was responsible for obtaining the $700 contribution from the Sons of Norway for production of the map, and still had time to help print courses, and enter results in the computer at the meet.

Gary McCausland built the start area and made all the signs, and helped with course-printing, and setting out markers; Arnold Kay spent many hours programming the computer and making awards before the meet, and operating the computer the weekend of the meet; Don Denbo (despite a badly swollen ankle that kept him out of the competition the second day) organized and trained a finely tuned finish crew.

Sture Carlsson spent many days designing and checking twelve courses, which required several trips to Snoqualmie Pass. Since the final map didn't get back from the printer until about 10 days before the meet, he had to do all of this with a bad color xerox of the field map.
Doug and Katie Sprugel handled pre-registration, and had to put up with a mailbox full of entries for the meet; Joe Abad and his family put together an excellent barbecue on Saturday evening.

Special thanks go to Larry and Sara Mae Berman from Cambridge, MA and Bob Anglin from L.A. who pitched in and worked as hard at taking up the dance stage as any of the COC members who worked on those 50 sheets of plywood.

Last, but certainly not least, thanks to everyone else who helped out: Eileen Williams, who coordinated the t-shirt printing; Robert Miller, who negotiated the permit fee with Burlington Northern, and who, with Sandy Miller, helped assemble the meet program. Roger Sharp, who did publicity for the meet; and Harvey Friedman, who got ads for the program. And everyone who helped on the days of the meet: Rick Breseman, Scott Coble, Ardis Dull, Bob Forbes, Mary Forbes, Harvey Friedman, Robert Miller, Sandy Kish Miller, Sal Munoz, Bob Rein, Erin Renshaw, Mike Tharp, Peggy Tharp, Rachel Tharp, and Anne York.

Several members of Cascade and Nisqually Orienteering Clubs attended the British Columbia Orienteering Championships held on Vancouver Island on August 30 and 31. The first day was an individual event, and the second day was a relay. The weather was perfect for orienteering, not too hot and not too cold. Thetis Lake, the site of the individual event, is typical Pacific Northwest vegetation, in other words, salal, salal, and more salal.

Saturday's courses were challenging, especially control 3 on course 7, where several people had to dodge a nest of angry bees to get to the marker. Sunday's relay was held on the grounds of Royal Roads Military Academy, located on the shores of Esquimalt Lagoon about five miles from Victoria. The weather was again perfect for orienteering, with a cool breeze coming in off the lagoon. The forest was a bit more open than at Thetis, although there were some thick spots. Fortunately, no one encountered any bees.
The American contingent did pretty well in the standings, and it was an enjoyable weekend in Canada.

This year's annual meeting and election of officers will be held on November 13th from 6:45-8:45 p.m. at the University Branch Library on the corner of N.E. 50th St. and Roosevelt. Mike Schuh will present a slide show about the Western U.S. Orienteering Championships (Nederland, Colorado), the B.C. Championships (Vancouver Island, B.C.) and the Washington State Championships (Trollhaugen).

In November, the Cascade Orienteering Club will hold a novel type of orienteering event: bus orienteering. Organized as a large scale score event, it will test the ability of the participants to use city street maps and the Metro bus system to navigate around the city.
Registration Information Start and Finish will be at Westlake Mall (4th & Pine in downtown Seattle).

Schedule 8:30 - 9:30 a.m. Registration 9:45 a.m. Last minute instructions and announcements 10:00 a.m. Mass start 1:30 p.m. Return by this time to avoid penalty points 2:00 p.m. All entrants must be back at Westlake Mall 2:15 p.m. Awards Ceremony
Entry Fees $2.00 Individual $2.00 Wheelchair (wheelchair entrants should have an assistant) $3.00 Team (2 or 3 people per team; only 1 wheelchair per team)

Bus Fare In addition to the registration fee, participants must supply their own bus fare. You may purchase an all-day pass for $1.00 from any bus driver. If you have a regular Metro pass, you may use it.

Instructions Your objective is to accumulate as many points as possible within the time limit. Each control is assigned a point value based on its distance and/or difficulty. It is usually not possible to visit every control within the time limit, so you must choose which points to visit. You may visit the controls in any order. Penalty points will be assessed if you return past the time limit.

You will be given a list of control descriptions. Each control description will consist of the name and address of a retail business in Seattle. You will also be asked to answer some question about the business in addition to punching in.
You must either walk/run or use a bus to get from one control point to the next.
You must return to the Finish area by 2:00 p.m. Penalty points will be assessed for anyone returning after 1:30 p.m.
Your score will be figured by adding the points from each control you visited. The person or team with the highest total will be declared the winner in each division.
This event is being run to gain publicity for orienteering and for the Cascade Orienteering Club. Therefore, one extra rule: no team may have more than one orienteering club member on it.
For additional information, call Mike Schuh at 783-3960.

Please check the date on the mailing label to see when your membership expires. Renew promptly if you want to continue receiving Bearing 315. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Pat Albright, Kurt Blackstad, Jo Brown, Georgie Friedenberg, Judy Hill family, Jack Raidy, Melanie Wheeler, and P.M. Greenspan.

Some members of Ellensburg and Sammamish Orienteering Clubs purchase "newsletter only" memberships from COC. These people should renew their memberships through their own clubs rather than through COC.

For those of you who want to find out about orienteering meets in other parts of the country, Cascade exchanges newsletters with clubs in New England, New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Delaware, Alabama, Kansas, Missouri, Texas, Colorado, Oregon, California, and British Columbia, Canada. Call Harvey Friedman (784-2774) if you want to borrow a newsletter to read about other clubs, or if you want current information from them.

Trollhaugen: Norwegian for "troll hill." I didn't see the trolls. I saw the hills. At the awards ceremony on Sunday afternoon, Liv Halvorsen was reported as saying "this isn't orienteering" when she had finished her course. Maybe she saw the trolls.

There was some deadfall, grassy fields, underbrush and dry river beds. Dry river beds in the Cascades! Near the wettest city in the country! This is a part of the Cascades new to me. It is, however, just as scenic and wild as the rest. Several people saw deer (probably SOC's Bob Rein bounding down a hillside). At one control was a beautiful waterfall. I saw an abundance of elk sign. You see a lot when you're on the course for three hours. Can you imagine people rushing through all this beauty in less than an hour? Crazy.

Even crazier was doing it again the next day. I didn't talk to one person on Day 2 who wasn't sore. Oh, yes I did. Alexander Rybak. Five months old, hanging out in Mom's front snuggle pack. Actually, he might have been sore. He didn't say.

About 110 people pre-registered for Trollhaugen. A lot of people registered on the day of the race. I saw many people milling about the popular spots: the free refreshments, the results board, the barbecue, the portable toilets. For people who like statistics:

Of the pre-registered: 35% were COC members 24% were Canadians 19% were Washingtonians from outside the Puget Sound area 8% were from the U.S. outside of Washington Participants represented 17 orienteering clubs Two people were injured on the course (sprained ankles)

Sara Mae and Larry Berman, publishers of Orienteering North America magazine, each took home a trophy. I heard they were interested by the news of snow at the Nisqually O' Club's Tenalquot meet in February of this year. Though the weather at Trollhaugen was warm and sunny, if they had stayed two more days they may have seen snow in Snoqualmie Pass.

Colorful club president Robert Miller presented the awards on Sunday, his mind tripping over Sweden and Norway each time he informed the audience where certain winners were from. Quick as he is, he caught on to his little glitch and introduced Sara Mae Berman as "from wherever it is back east." This is not a direct quote, but it may as well be.
All in all, the meet went as smoothly as contour lines printed on a map, and I'm sure meet director Debbie Newell is happy.

I'd like everyone to thank Debbie Newell for the job she did putting out the superb last issue of the COC newsletter and hope that all the others she does next year as regular newsletter editor turn out as well. We need more people to step forward and offer to accomplish things for the club.

Most of the membership of COC enjoys participating in o' meets; few members enjoy the administrative aspects of the sport. However, orienteering in this country is a sport run by volunteers, which means people are needed to design courses, arrange for maps to be made, schedule events, register, time, and teach others how to do the above. Volunteers can learn new skills, make new friends, and share the camaraderie of putting on a well-attended, successful meet. You don't have to be a "good" or proficient orienteer, just enthusiastic. Several returned surveys from last spring had comments about the officers being "elitist" or cliquish; those comments hurt those who worked at meets or who served on the board. If you want proof that they are not cliquish, just volunteer to help out and see how eagerly you will be welcomed. Newer members can learn from more experienced ones how to do most of the above-mentioned jobs, but we dearly need someone (anyone competent) to help the current publicity coordinator get our schedule published REGULARLY in the local media (particularly the Times and the P.I.). We need this ongoing name familiarity so that the club can grow. Help the soon-to-be-elected board members. Better yet, be one. If you enjoy the sport, please volunteer to help out.

I would rather see 100 or more people come to each meet and have 75 treat it as a 10km run and just leave afterward than have only 25 friendly people show up. I feel that if we can introduce enough people to the sport, some will stay and become involved. A big problem is keeping the interest of those who do come fairly often. To this end, I think that the four clubs in the state of Washington should coordinate their schedules so that in each and every month of the year there will be at least one meet on a 1:2500 to 1:5000 scale map, primarily to introduce beginners to the sport in a non-threatening locale and also at least one meet on a 1:10,000 to 1:24,000 scale map, so that the non-competitive but no-longer beginners won't become bored and drop out. If anyone agrees with anything I've advocated here, come to the annual meeting in November and make your voice heard.
Harvey Friedman Membership Coordinator Former President & Former Newsletter Editor

THERE IS A HEAVEN FOR VOLUNTEERS
Many will be shocked to find When the day of judgement nears That there's a special place in Heaven Set aside for volunteers
Furnished with big recliners Satin couches and footstools Where there's no Committee Chairman No group leaders, no car pools
No eager team that needs a coach No bazaar and no bake sale There will be nothing to staple Not one thing to fold or mail
Telephone lists will be outlawed But a finger snap will bring Cool drinks and gourmet dinners And rare treats fit for a king
You ask "Who'll serve those privileged few And work for all they're worth?" Why, all those who reaped the benefits And not once volunteered on Earth

TROLLHAUGEN T-SHIRTS Several Trollhaugen t-shirts remain for those of you who didn't get one. We are offering a special post-meet rate of $6.00 apiece for these lovely aqua shirts emblazoned with an orienteering troll. To purchase a shirt, please call Debbie Newell at (206) 488-7471.
